planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Start seeds on the surface of a moist, fine-textured germination mix at 70–75°F; seeds need light to germinate so do not cover. Transplant into full sun after last frost, spacing 12–18 in apart in well-drained soil enriched with compost. Angelonia is heat- and drought-tolerant once established and rarely needs deadheading, as spent florets drop cleanly. Pinch stem tips early to encourage branching and a fuller plant shape.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow outdoors in early spring; blooms appear the second year from seed.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly, just barely covering them, since light aids germination; thin or transplant to 12–18 in spacing. Balloon flowers are late to emerge in spring — mark their location to avoid accidentally digging the fleshy taproot. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the blooming period through summer, and avoid dividing since the taproot resents disturbance. Plants are long-lived and improve in beauty over many years with minimal care.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; surface-sow without covering as seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix and provide light at 65–70°F; seedlings emerge in 2–3 weeks and grow slowly their first season. Plant in consistently moist to wet soil — rain gardens, pond edges, and low-lying areas suit this plant perfectly. Brilliant red spikes appear in midsummer to early fall and are hummingbird magnets. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years), so allow self-sowing or divide offsets each spring to maintain the colony.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into the garden 2–4 weeks before last frost, as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and can be transplanted while still small. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral-to-slightly-alkaline soil; heavy clay and overwet conditions lead to crown rot. Deadhead spent flowers continuously to prevent early seed set and extend the blooming season well into summer. Plants often decline in intense summer heat but may rebloom in fall if sheared back and kept watered.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost while soil is still cool; succession sow every 3 weeks for extended bloom.

directions: Direct sow on the soil surface or barely cover; seeds germinate best at cool soil temperatures and seedlings dislike transplanting. Thin ruthlessly to 9–12 in because overcrowded plants bloom poorly and are prone to powdery mildew in humid conditions. Clarkia thrives on neglect in poor, well-drained soil — fertilizing too heavily causes lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants naturally set seed and die when summer heat arrives; in mild-winter climates, sow in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist germination mix and provide light at 65–75°F; germination is slow and uneven over 10–14 days. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; excellent salt and drought tolerance makes this ideal for coastal and hot dry sites. The silvery white, felted foliage is the ornamental feature — pinch off yellow flower buds when they appear to keep plants neat and maintain vigorous leaf growth. In zones 8–11 dusty miller overwinters as a short-lived perennial.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early fall so plants overwinter as rosettes and bloom in spring alongside tulips and daffodils.

directions: Scatter seeds on the soil surface or barely cover them; they germinate best in cool conditions and seedlings overwinter as low rosettes in mild climates. Forget-me-nots self-sow prolifically once established — let plants go to seed, then pull them after flowering to control spread. The sky-blue flowers are traditionally combined with spring bulbs in beds and create a charming filler under shrubs in partial shade. Remove spent plants when temperatures rise, as they become straggly and die back in summer heat.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in loose, well-drained germination mix; plants develop a deep taproot early so pot on before roots become crowded. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil — gaura thrives on neglect and declines in fertile, wet conditions. The wand-like stems bearing butterfly-shaped white or pink flowers sway gracefully all season without deadheading. Cut plants back to 6 in after the first heavy bloom flush to encourage a tidy second flush through autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; they germinate readily at 65–75°F and grow quickly into compact transplants. Plant in sharply drained, even poor soil in the hottest, sunniest location available — gazania excels in sandy or gravelly beds where other plants fail. Flowers close at night and on overcast days, but modern hybrid varieties stay open longer than older types. Drought tolerance is excellent once established; overwatering and heavy clay soils are the most common causes of failure.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds vertically, pointed end down, barely covered, as they are sensitive to orientation; maintain 70–75°F for germination. Plant so the crown sits slightly above soil level to prevent crown rot, which is the most common cause of plant failure. Water at the base rather than overhead; gerberas prefer a dry-wet-dry cycle rather than constantly moist soil. Deadhead spent flowers by cutting the stem to the base to encourage continuous bloom from spring through fall in mild climates.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants typically bloom in their second year from seed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep; cold stratification is not required but may speed germination. Established globe thistle is extremely drought-tolerant and thrives in poor, sandy, or chalky well-drained soil — fertile, moist soil causes lax growth and flopping. The spiky steel-blue globe flowers are excellent for fresh cutting and dry perfectly for everlasting arrangements if cut just before fully open. Divide clumps in spring every 4–5 years using a sharp spade; they are slow to re-establish so divisions should be generous.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 4–6 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep directly in the garden or start indoors; unlike true perennial rudbeckia, heliopsis often blooms robustly in its first season from an early start. Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil; plants tolerate heat and short dry periods better than helenium. Deadhead regularly throughout summer to keep the golden-yellow, semi-double or double flowers coming continuously from July through September. Divide clumps every 3 years in spring or fall to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant only after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; germination is slow (14–28 days) and benefits from bottom heat at 70–80°F consistently. Transplant into rich, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; heat brings out the intensely vanilla-scented, deep purple flower clusters. Pinch tips once or twice before transplanting to produce bushier plants with more flowering heads. In zones 10–11 heliotrope is a shrubby perennial; elsewhere take cuttings in late summer to overwinter prized fragrant selections indoors.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after nicking the seed coat and soaking 24 hours; transplant after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Nick or file seed coats and soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination rates significantly. Plant in full sun in moist, fertile soil — hardy hibiscus is native to wetlands and performs best with consistent moisture but tolerates average garden beds with regular irrigation. This is a late riser in spring; the shoots emerge much later than other perennials, so mark plants to avoid accidental disturbance. The dinner-plate-sized flowers in white, pink, and crimson open each morning and close by evening, with new blooms produced daily from midsummer through frost.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed into sharply drained soil.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, gritty mix without covering, as they need light to germinate; avoid overwatering at all stages. Plant in the hottest, driest, sunniest spot in the garden — sandy, rocky, or gravelly soil is ideal, and root rot is the main cause of failure in clay or poorly drained sites. The magenta to purple daisy-like flowers cover the succulent mats nearly all summer and reopen only in bright sunlight. Hardy ice plant spreads steadily to make a weed-suppressing groundcover on slopes and rock gardens.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4–6 weeks in a moist medium in the refrigerator before sowing indoors; germination is improved but still irregular. Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; this native prairie plant tolerates occasional wet feet and dry spells once established. Cutting stems back by half in June (Chelsea chop) reduces ultimate height and prevents the need for staking without sacrificing the domed, mauve-pink flower clusters beloved by butterflies. Seedheads are ornamentally interesting into winter and provide food for goldfinches.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed in spring.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep and maintain even moisture at 65–75°F until germination. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — good drainage is critical, especially in winter, as plants rot in waterlogged conditions. The torch-like flower spikes transition from red or orange in bud to yellow as the florets open, creating a two-toned effect that attracts hummingbirds throughout summer and fall. In zones 5–6 tie the leaves together and mulch deeply around the crown before the first hard freeze to protect the growing point.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; most gardeners use purchased transplants due to slow, uneven germination.

directions: Sow seeds after lightly scarifying or nicking the seed coat; soak overnight to improve germination, which is slow and can take 4–6 weeks at 70–80°F. Transplant in full sun after all frost risk has passed; lantana thrives in heat, drought, and poor soil and blooms most freely under mild stress. The multicolored flower clusters change color as they age, giving plants a jewel-like effect; deadhead clusters as they fade to keep new flowers coming. Perennial in zones 9–11 where it can become invasive; grow as an annual elsewhere.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in spring as soon as the soil can be worked; they establish quickly and bloom the first season.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep with the flat side down in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; rich or wet soil leads to floppy growth. Liatris is unique in opening its flowers from the top of the spike downward, prolonging the bloom period for 3–4 weeks in midsummer. The purple to rose-pink spikes are outstanding cut flowers and highly attractive to butterflies and bees; stems last 10–14 days in a vase. Clumps multiply steadily; divide every 3–4 years in spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain strong bloom.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost date; lisianthus requires a very long lead time and is challenging from seed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny, pelleted seeds on sterile, fine-textured mix; do not cover as seeds need light and maintain steady 70–75°F. Seedlings grow extremely slowly for the first 8–10 weeks, so patience is essential — do not overwater at this stage as damping off is common. Transplant carefully after last frost into rich, well-drained, alkaline-leaning soil in full sun. The ruffled, rose-like blooms in white, purple, pink, and bicolor make lisianthus one of the finest cut flowers; harvest stems when 2–3 buds per stem have opened.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; lobelia has extremely tiny seeds and a slow early growth rate.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-like seeds on moist fine-textured mix; do not cover, as they need light to germinate, and use a spray mist to avoid washing them away. Grow seedlings on at cool temperatures (60–65°F) once germinated; pot up in small clusters of 3–5 seedlings rather than separating individuals. Lobelia blooms best in cool weather and partial shade in hot climates, often slowing or stopping in midsummer heat — shear back by half and water regularly for rebloom as fall approaches. The vivid blue, trailing types are classic basket and container plants.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost in early spring; also sow in fall in zones 7–10 for spring bloom.

directions: Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil and rake in 1/8 in deep; nigella resents transplanting and germinates best in cool soil. Thin seedlings to 9–12 in to prevent overcrowding — plants are quick to grow and will self-sow freely if allowed to set the inflated seed pods. The blue or white flowers are surrounded by thread-like bracts that give the plant its misty appearance; after bloom the ornamental balloon-like pods are equally attractive and excellent for dried arrangements. Make succession sowings every 3–4 weeks until early summer for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in fine-textured germination mix at 60–65°F; avoid higher temperatures as heat inhibits germination. Plant outdoors in cool weather in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade where summers are hot. Nemesia blooms prolifically in cool conditions with cheerful two-toned flowers in a huge range of colors; it typically falters in summer heat so shear back and move containers to a cooler spot. Plants may revive and rebloom in fall when temperatures drop again.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; plants bloom in year two from seed but transplants purchased in spring bloom the first year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination. Plant in moist to average soil in full sun; established plants spread aggressively by underground rhizomes and may require annual division to prevent them from overtaking the border. The name refers to the curious behavior of individual florets staying in place when moved along the square stem. The pink to lilac spikes bloom in late summer and fall when pollinators are seeking food before winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; seeds need cool soil and germinate best when sown directly outdoors.

directions: Surface-sow seeds in early spring on well-prepared, well-drained soil; seeds need cool temperatures and light, so press firmly without covering. Oriental poppies bloom for a brief but spectacular 2–3 weeks in late spring to early summer with crepe-paper petals in orange, red, pink, or white up to 6 in across. Plants go dormant after bloom leaving a bare spot — interplant with late-emerging annuals like baby's breath or dahlias to fill the gap. Divide clumps in late summer while dormant; established plants dislike being moved during the growing season.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in well-drained germination mix; seedlings grow quickly and should be potted on before becoming root-bound. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil as soon as the risk of hard frost has passed; osteospermum thrives in cool weather and slows during peak summer heat. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continuous bloom and pinch stem tips to promote branching early in the season. In zones 9–11 it may survive as a short-lived perennial; take stem cuttings in late summer to perpetuate favorite selections.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist fine-textured mix and provide light at 70–80°F; germination occurs within 7–14 days. Pentas is one of the best butterfly and hummingbird plants for hot, humid gardens, blooming continuously from transplant to frost without deadheading. Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil and fertilize monthly to sustain the heavy flower production. Shear plants back by one-third in midsummer if they become leggy; they recover quickly in heat and resume flowering within 2–3 weeks.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant 2 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in a well-drained mix; germination is reliable at 60–70°F and seedlings grow quickly. Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil — scabiosa performs best in cool to moderate temperatures and fades in intense summer heat. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continuous bloom from early summer until fall; flowers are excellent for cutting with long, wiry stems and last 7–10 days in a vase. In zones 7–10 try sowing in fall for earlier spring bloom before heat sets in.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 14–16 weeks before last frost after surface-sowing and cold-stratifying at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist, sterile mix and cold-stratify at 35–40°F for 3–4 weeks to break dormancy, then move to 65°F for germination. Grow on at cool temperatures (55–60°F) since heat causes etiolation; transplant into partial shade in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in early spring. Primroses bloom best in cool, moist conditions — they are outstanding spring plants but typically go dormant in summer heat. Divide crowded clumps after bloom in spring and replant divisions in refreshed, compost-amended soil.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; plants may bloom the first fall from an early sowing or establish and bloom their second year.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained soil; germination is fast and reliable. Plant in full sun in poor to average, very well-drained soil — rose campion thrives under dry, sunny conditions that would stress many perennials. The intensely magenta-crimson flowers glow against the woolly, silver-white felted stems and leaves, which are ornamental throughout the growing season. Individual plants are short-lived (2–3 years) but self-sow abundantly so colonies persist indefinitely; thin or transplant self-sown seedlings to prevent overcrowding.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier for bloom the first season.

directions: Press seeds onto moist soil surface or cover very lightly; they need light to germinate and germinate readily in 7–14 days. Unlike Rudbeckia hirta, R. triloba is a true biennial-to-short-lived-perennial that produces abundant small golden-yellow flowers with a dark center from midsummer through hard frost. Plant in full sun to partial shade in average garden soil; plants self-sow prolifically and naturalize easily in meadow and prairie plantings. Deadhead some stems to extend the season and let others set seed to perpetuate the colony.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant 2–3 weeks before last frost as plants tolerate light frost.

directions: Sow seeds in the dark (cover lightly and keep the tray in darkness) at 60–70°F; darkness improves germination for salpiglossis. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the petunia-like flowers with intricate veining in yellow, red, purple, maroon, and bicolor thrive in cool weather. Provide light support with twiggy stakes since plants can flop in wind; pinch tips once for bushier plants. Plants decline in summer heat — treat them as a spring and fall flower in warm climates or plant where afternoon shade moderates temperatures.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring around last frost date; cold stratification for 4 weeks improves germination of seed.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds for 4 weeks before sowing; established divisions establish more reliably than seed-grown plants and can be done in spring or fall. Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, average to rich soil; plants tolerate wet conditions and make excellent rain garden plants. The three-petaled flowers in purple, blue, pink, or white open only for a single morning but are produced in rapid succession over many weeks. Shear the entire plant to 8 in after the main bloom flush to stimulate fresh foliage and a second round of flowers in fall.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 4–6 weeks; transplant after last frost.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist medium at 35–40°F for 4–6 weeks before sowing indoors; germination is slow but improved by stratification. Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or loamy soil; Stokes' aster is native to the American Southeast and tolerates heat and humidity well but dislikes wet winter conditions. The large, cornflower-like blooms in blue, lavender, or white appear from early summer to fall and are excellent for cutting. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; plants spread slowly and are well-behaved in the border.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant can go out 2–4 weeks before last frost as plants are frost-tolerant.

directions: Sow seeds 1/8 in deep in well-drained germination mix; germination is fast and reliable at 65–70°F. Plant in full sun in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; sweet William is technically a biennial that flowers in its second year, but modern annual varieties bloom the first year from an early indoor start. The domed, fragrant flower clusters in vivid shades of red, pink, white, and bicolor appear in late spring and early summer. Deadhead spent clusters promptly to extend the blooming period; plants self-sow readily to maintain a colony from year to year.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Surface-sow tiny seeds on moist mix without covering; they need light to germinate at 70–75°F. Torenia is one of the best flowering annuals for shaded and part-shaded spots in hot, humid climates where impatiens are lost to downy mildew. The bicolored, snapdragon-like flowers in purple, pink, white, or yellow bloom profusely without deadheading throughout summer. Keep soil consistently moist, as plants wilt quickly when dry but recover readily; fertilize monthly with a balanced liquid feed.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost after cold-stratifying for 1–2 weeks; often blooms from seed the first year.

directions: Cold-stratify seeds in moist towels for 1–2 weeks at 35–40°F, then sow indoors at 65–75°F; germination remains erratic over 14–28 days. Plant in full sun in poor to average, well-drained soil; this South American native is drought-tolerant and naturalizes readily once established. The wiry, branching stems carry clusters of small purple flowers from midsummer through frost at an unusual height that allows it to weave transparently through other plants. Grown as an annual north of zone 7, it self-sows prolifically even in cold climates and seedlings replace killed parent plants.

planting.note: In zones 7–10 sow in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost for winter and spring bloom; in colder zones direct sow in very early spring as soon as soil can be worked.

directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seeds on well-prepared, fine-textured soil; seeds need light and cool temperatures (50–60°F) to germinate — warm soil prevents germination. Plants grow as basal rosettes through winter in mild climates before sending up slender stems topped with large, tissue-paper flowers in spring. Iceland poppies are cool-season plants that stop flowering when summer heat arrives; in cold climates treat them as an early-spring annual with fall-sown or indoor-started transplants set out very early. Harvest cut flowers when buds are just beginning to crack open color and sear stem ends in boiling water to maximize vase life.
